blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (۱)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (۲)
These are the signs of the wise Book, (2)
هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّلْمُحْسِنِينَ (۳)
a guidance and mercy for the virtuous, (3)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
who maintain the prayer, pay the zakat, and are certain of the Hereafter. (4)

Key Points

Out of the twenty-nine Surahs of the Qur'an that begin with the disjointed letters (al-Huroof al-Muqatta'ah), in twenty-four instances, the greatness of the Qur'an is mentioned immediately after these letters. Perhaps this indicates that the Qur'an is composed of the very letters of the alphabet available to you, yet none of you can produce anything like it. Most writers do not consider their books free of flaws, and because of the shortcomings and problems in their works, they apologize to the reader and welcome suggestions and criticisms. Only Allah explicitly states concerning His Book: «الكتاب الحكیم» (The Wise Book). All of its verses are firm and based on wisdom. It is a steadfast and impregnable Book into which no flaw or defect can enter. Prayer (Salat) is comprehensive of all spiritual perfections, such as purification (Taharah), recitation of the Qur'an, affirmation of Monotheism (Tawhid), Prophethood (Nubuwwah), and Guardianship (Wilayah), remembrance (Dhikr) and supplication (Du'a), salutation (Salam), standing (Qiyam), bowing (Ruku'), prostration (Sujud), and attention to the Truth (Allah); and Zakat compensates for all material deficiencies. In the Qur'an, the concept of "Zakat" is broader than what is defined in jurisprudence (Fiqh), as it includes general financial aid in addition to the jurisprudential Zakat. In one place, Allah calls the Qur'an a source of guidance for the pious, «هدىً للمتّقین» (185), and in another place, He considers it a source of guidance and good tidings for the believers, «هدىً و بشرى للمؤمنین» (186). In this Surah, He counts the Qur'an as a source of guidance and mercy for the doers of good, «هدىً و رحمة للمحسنین». Thus, the Qur'an encompasses the three stages of perfection: it is a source of guidance, good tidings, and mercy (187).

185) Al-Baqarah, 2. 186) An-Naml, 2. 187) Tafsir Namuneh, concerning the Ayah.

Messages

1- Guidance and instruction must be based on wisdom. «آیات الكتاب الحكیم هدىً»

2- The Qur'an is the essence of guidance and mercy, and it has no deficiency or inadequacy in this regard. «هدىً و رحمة» (The words «هدىً» and «رحمة» are presented in the infinitive form, which indicates hyperbole (188).)

3- Instruction and guidance must be accompanied by mercy and affection. «هدىً و رحمة»

4- The doers of good are prepared to accept the truth. «هدىً للمحسنین»

5- Prayer (Salat) and Zakat are inseparable from one another. «یقیمون الصّلاة و یؤتون الزّكاة»

6- Establishing prayer and paying Zakat must be a continuous practice. («یقیمون» and «یؤتون» are in the present tense form, which indicates continuity.)

7- Since Islam is a comprehensive religion, its duties are also comprehensive and all-encompassing. Physical duty: "Prayer," financial duty: "Zakat," and spiritual duty: "Certainty (Yaqin)." «الصّلاة، الزّكاة، یوقنون»

8- Connection with Allah (establishing prayer) and connection with people (paying Zakat) are valuable when accompanied by faith in the Hereafter. «و هم بالآخرة هم یوقنون»

9- The doer of good is one who pays attention to both economic issues and spiritual issues. «للمحسنین الذین یقیمون... یؤتون» 188) Sometimes they say: So-and-so is sweet-spoken, and sometimes they say: So-and-so is sugar, which expresses that his entire being, movements, speech, and gaze are wholly sweet.

Explanations

Portrait of Surah Luqman

This Surah is one of the Meccan Surahs and is named Luqman because of the name Luqman, which appears only twice in the entire Qur'an, both times in this Surah. It is the sixth of the seven Surahs of the "Lamats" (those beginning with L) which starts with the disjointed letters «الم». The content of Surah Luqman can be summarized in the following points: 1- Stating the greatness and importance of the Qur'an in guiding humanity. 2- Dividing people into the doers of good and the arrogant, and describing their destinies. 3- Mentioning some scientific miracles of the Qur'an, such as the law of gravity and the pairing (sexuality) of plants. 4- The wise advice and admonitions of Luqman to his son. 5- Arguments for faith in the Origin (Allah) and the Return (Resurrection). 6- Stating the exclusive knowledge of Allah, such as the time of death and the establishment of the Hour (Qiyamah).

Ayah

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥)
Those follow their Lord’s guidance, and it is they who are the felicitous. (5)

Key Points

It is inferred from this Ayah that salvation is achieved based on the divine grace and guidance of the Lord, which, of course, is also granted to man in the shadow of effort and struggle, just as we read elsewhere: «اِنّ الّذین جاهدوا فینا لَنَهدیَنّهم سُبلنا و اِنّ اللّه لَمَع المحسنین»(189) Those who strove and struggled in Our way, We showed them the path, and indeed, Allah is with the doers of good.

189) Al-Ankabut, 69.

Messages

1- The doers of good benefit from divine guidance. «أولئك على هُدىً من ربّهم»

2- Guidance is among the affairs of Lordship. «هدىً من ربّهم»

3- The divine grace for good deeds is a favor from Allah. «للمحسنین... هُدىً من ربّهم»

4- Establishing prayer and paying Zakat prepares the ground for benefiting from the guidance of the Quran. «الّذین یقيمون... و یؤتون... أولئك على هُدىً»

5- Salvation is exclusive to those who are devoted to prayer, Zakat, and certainty in the Hereafter. «أولئك هم المفلحون»

Ayah

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْتَرِى لَهْوَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٦)
Among the people is he who buys diversionary talk that he may lead [people] astray from Allah’s way without any knowledge, and he takes it in derision. For such there is a humiliating punishment. (6)

Key Points

The word lahw (لَهْو) means something that distracts a person from an important goal. Lahw al-Hadith (لهو الحدیث) refers to vain speech that distracts a person from the truth, such as superstitious tales and stories that lead a person to corruption and sin. This deviation is sometimes due to the content of the speech and sometimes due to its means and accompaniments, such as music and related matters.(190)

A person named Nadr ibn Harith, who traveled from Hijaz to Iran, learned Iranian legends—such as the tales of Rustam and Isfandiyar and the history of kings—and recounted them to the Arab people, saying: If Muhammad tells you the stories of 'Ad and Thamud, I will also recount the stories and news of the non-Arabs (Ajam). This Ayah was revealed and deemed his action a method of deviation.

Some commentators, regarding the occasion of the revelation of this Ayah, have said that people would buy singing slave girls to sing for them, thereby distracting people from hearing the Quran recited by the Prophet. The above Ayah was revealed in condemnation of such individuals.(191)

This Ayah points to one of the most important factors leading to misguidance, which is false speech. In other verses of the Quran, some other factors of people's misguidance are mentioned, which include:

A: The Taghut (tyrant), who sometimes misguides people through humiliation, "استخفّ قومه" (He made his people light-headed)(192), and sometimes through threat, "لاجعلنّك من المسجونین" (I will surely make you among the imprisoned)(193).

B: Satan, who misguides man through his whispers. "یرید الشّیطان ان یضلّهم" (Satan desires to lead them astray)(194).

C: The deviated scholar or artist who misguides others using his knowledge and art. "و اضلّهم السّامرى" (And the Samiri led them astray)(195).

D: Those possessing power and wealth, who use power and wealth to turn people away from the truth. "انّا اَطعنا سادتنا و كبراءنا فاضلّونا السبیلا" (Indeed, we obeyed our masters and our great ones, and they led us astray from the way)(196).

E: Speakers and singers who entertain people and distract them from the truth and reality. "یشرى لهو الحدیث لیضلّ عن سبیل اللّه" (He purchases diversionary speech to mislead [others] from the way of Allah).

190) Tafsir Al-Mizan. 191) Tafsir Namuneh. 192) Az-Zukhruf, 54. 193) Ash-Shu'ara, 29. 194) An-Nisa, 60. 195) Ta Ha, 85. 196) Al-Ahzab, 67.

Messages

1- Investing in cultural warfare and aggression against the truth has a long history. "و من النّاس من یشترى لهو الحدیث"

2- Whatever stands opposite wisdom is lahw (diversion) and prevents reaching perfection. "الكتاب الحكیم - لهو الحدیث" (It is astonishing that people abandon the free wisdom offered by the infallible Prophet and seek to purchase diversion from irresponsible individuals.)

3- The tools of those who oppose the path of Allah are not logic and wisdom, but vain and baseless speech. "من یشترى لهوالحدیث لیضلّ عن سبیل اللّه"

4- Purchasing means of diversion (lahw) and things that prevent a person from achieving perfection is a sign of ignorance and foolishness. "یشترى لهو الحدیث... بغیر علم"

5- The proportionality of punishment to the deed is a sign of justice. Those who insult the truth through mockery will have a humiliating and disgraceful punishment. "یتّخذها هزواً... لهم عذاب مهین"

Avoiding Vain Speech and Assemblies

From the perspective of Islam, melodies (āhang) that are appropriate for assemblies of immorality, debauchery, and sin, and which stimulate carnal desires, are forbidden (haram). However, if a melody does not have the aforementioned corrupting effects, the famous opinion of the jurists is that listening to it is permissible (majāz).

Imam Baqir عليه السلام said: Singing (ghinā) is among the sins for which Allah has promised the Fire. He then recited this Ayah: "و من النّاس من یشترى لهو الحدیث..."(197). Therefore, ghinā is a major sin (kabirah), because a major sin is defined as a sin for which the promise of punishment is given in the Quran.

In Surah Al-Hajj, we read: "واجتنبوا قول الزّور" (And avoid false speech)(198). Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: "The meaning of qawl az-zūr (false speech) is singing and ghinā."(199)

It is also narrated from Imam Sadiq and Imam Ridha علیهم السلام that one of the manifestations of Lahw al-Hadith in Surah Luqman is ghinā.

In narrations, we read:(200)

  • Ghinā nurtures the spirit of hypocrisy and brings poverty and misery.
  • Female singers, the one who pays them, and the one who spends that money are cursed, just as teaching singing women is forbidden (haram).
  • A house in which ghinā takes place is not safe from painful death and calamity; prayers are not answered therein, and angels do not enter it.(201)

The Destructive Effects of Ghinā

  1. Promoting moral corruption, moving away from the spirit of piety, and turning toward lusts and sins, to the extent that one of the leaders of Banu Umayyah (despite all his own corruption) confessed: Ghinā reduces modesty, increases lust, shatters personality, and does the same thing that wine does.

Some people enter a world of carelessness and indifference by drinking wine or consuming drugs, while others, by listening to the vain and lustful speech of a singer, ignore their sense of honor (ghayrat) and live in a world of indifference.

  1. Negligence of Allah, negligence of duty, negligence of the deprived, negligence of the future, negligence of capabilities and talents, negligence of enemies, negligence of the self and Satan.

Modern man, despite all his advancements in science and technology, burns in a fire originating from his own negligence.

Indeed, negligence of Allah leads man to the brink of animality, or even lower than that. "أولئك كالانعام بل هم أضلّ أولئك هم الغافلون" (Those are like livestock; rather, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless)(202).

Today, colonialists utilize all means, including the promotion of vulgar music through various devices and waves, to entertain and drug the younger generation for their colonial objectives.

  1. The harmful effects of provocative and vulgar music on the nervous system are hidden from no one. In the book The Effect of Music on the Psyche and Nerves, important points are mentioned regarding the end of life and the bad fate of musical instrument players, their affliction with various mental illnesses, sudden strokes, cardiovascular diseases, and undesirable stimulations, which are worthy of attention and contemplation for those who reflect.(203)

Dear Reader!

Allah created the world for humanity and humanity for spiritual perfection, growth, and proximity [to Him]. He subjugated the universe for us and compelled the angels to manage our affairs. He sent the Prophets and the Imams (Awliyā) for our guidance, and they proceeded to the point of death and martyrdom. He commanded the angels to prostrate to Adam, breathed His Divine Spirit into man, and granted man the best form and character. He deposited within him the capacity for infinite growth, equipped him with intellect, innate nature (fitrah), and various talents, and only upon His creation did He praise Himself. "فتبارك اللّه أحسن الخالقین" (So blessed is Allah, the best of creators)(204).

Should this crown jewel of existence, with all these material and spiritual advantages, place himself at the disposal of just anyone? Imam Kadhim عليه السلام said: To whatever sound you lend your ear, you become its slave. Is it not a pity that we should be the slave of some singer?!

Would you be willing to record every sound onto a cassette tape? Then how is it that we record every sound onto the tape of our brain? Life, thought, and the brain are trusts (amānāt) that Allah has given us to spend in the way He has specified. If we spend any of these trusts in a way other than the way of Allah, we have betrayed that trust, and we must be accountable on the Day of Judgment.

The Quran explicitly states in Surah Al-Isra: "انّ السّمع و البصر و الفؤاد كلّ اولئك كان عنه مسئولاً" (Indeed, the hearing, the sight and the heart—about all those [one] will be questioned)(205). The ear, the eye, and the heart will all be questioned. Yes, man must be the guardian of his eye, ear, and heart and not allow every call to enter them. In a narration, we read: The heart is the sanctuary (haram) of Allah; do not let anyone other than Allah enter it.(206)

Islam has provided us with appropriate and healthy ways to relieve fatigue and create vitality; it has recommended travel and journeying, exercise and swimming, diverse and beneficial work, visiting and socializing, scientific discussions and sharing experiences, connecting with the educated, and, most importantly, connecting with the Creator of existence and remembering Him, for He alone is the soother of hearts. Why should we occupy ourselves with pleasures whose end is humiliation and fire?

Imam Ali عليه السلام says: "لاخیر فى لذّةٍ من بعدها النّار" (There is no good in a pleasure after which is the Fire)(207). "مَن تَلذّد لمعاصى اللّه اَورثه اللّه ذلّاً" (Whoever seeks pleasure through disobedience to Allah, Allah will bequeath him humiliation)(208).

197) Kafi, Vol. 6, p. 431. 198) Al-Hajj, 30. 199) Zūr means falsehood, lie, and deviation from the truth, and ghinā is one of the manifestations of falsehood. 200) These narrations are found in the chapters on the prohibition of ghinā in Makasib Muharramah and books of narrations. 201) Mizan al-Hikmah. 202) Al-A'raf, 179. 203) Tafsir Namuneh, Vol. 17, pp. 19 - 27. 204) Al-Mu'minun, 14. 205) Al-Isra, 36. 206) Bihar, Vol. 67, p. 25. 207) Diwan of Imam Ali عليه السلام, p. 204. 208) Ghurar al-Hikam, 3565.

Ayah

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا وَلَّىٰ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا كَأَنَّ فِىٓ أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْرًا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (۷)
When Our signs are recited to him he turns away disdainfully, as if he had not heard them [at all], as if there were a deafness in his ears. So inform him of a painful punishment. (7)

Key Points

The word "وَقْر" (waqr) means heaviness or weight. Individuals who are dignified and serious are also called bā-waqār (dignified/composed). This Ayah implies that arrogant individuals are not even willing to listen to the word of truth, let alone listening to it, reflecting upon it, and then rejecting it if it were illogical.

Messages

1- Listening to vain and false speech removes a person's readiness to accept the truth. «لهو الحدیث... ولّى مستكبراً»

2- Some individuals, no matter what language they hear the divine verses in, still refuse to submit. «و اذا تُتلى علیه آیاتنا ولّى مستكبراً»

If the ear is yours and the lament is mine, That which certainly reaches nowhere is the cry.

3- The spirit of arrogance prevents the acceptance of truth and reality. «ولّى مُستكبراً»

4- Not listening to the word of truth is a sign of arrogance. «مُستكبراً كان لم یسمعها»

5- He who seeks false speech is not ready to hear the Quran. «یشترى لهو الحدیث... اذا تتلى علیه... لم یسمعها»

6- The arrogant deserve contempt. «كأنّ فى أُذنیه وقراً»

7- One of the methods of propagation and education used by the Quran is the use of examples and analogies. «كَأن... كَأَنّ...»

8- The one who does not accept the truth is like someone whose ears are both heavy (hard of hearing). «كأنّ فى اُذنیه وَقرا»

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (۸)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be gardens of bliss, (8)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (۹)
to remain in them [forever]—a true promise of Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (9)

Key Points

In the previous verses, it was stated that some people try to mislead the followers of truth with vain and useless talk. Having an arrogant spirit and refusing to listen to the heavenly verses, they mock the believers. Allah the Almighty, in this verse, comforts the believers by giving them the glad tidings of the blessings of Paradise.

Messages

1- Paradise is exclusive to the people of faith and righteous deeds. «انّ الّذین آمنوا و عملوا الصّالحات لهم جنّات...»

2- The humiliation and mockery by the arrogant are compensated for the true believers through divine promises and glad tidings. «انّ الّذین آمنوا... لهم جنّات...»

3- Faith accompanied by righteous deeds is a condition for benefiting from divine favors. «آمنوا و عملوا الصالحات لهم جنّات النعیم»

4- The arrogance and turning away of the opponents are fleeting, but the reward for the people of faith is eternal. «خالدین فیها»

5- We must believe in and take seriously the promises and rewards of Allah. «وعْداللّه حقّاً»

6- Divine power and wisdom are the guarantee behind His promises. «وعْداللّه حقّاً و هو العزیز الحكیم»

7- The power and might of Allah are realized based on His wisdom. «العزیزُ الحكیم» (Indeed, today the actions of many powerful people are not wise.)

Ayah

خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ (۱٠)
He created the heavens without any pillars that you may see, and cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and He has scattered in it every kind of animal. And We sent down water from the sky and caused every splendid kind [of plant] to grow in it. (10)

Key Points

Although every creature can be viewed from several angles, usually everything has a special importance and prominence in one direction and from one angle. In this Ayah, the important characteristic of the heavens and the planets is their suspension and their invisible pillars. The important characteristic of the mountains is stability and preventing the shaking of the earth. The important aspect of animals is their diversity and spread across the earth, and the important characteristic of plants is their pairing (duality) and their high value. This Ayah refers to several examples of the scientific miracle (I'jaz) of the Quran at a time when humanity did not even have the imagination or conjecture of it: One is the reference to invisible pillars and levers for the stability of the planets and celestial bodies. That is, the force of gravity and the centrifugal force, two powers that are the secret to the rotation of the planets in their orbits. Another miracle is the reference to protecting the earth from shaking through the establishment of mountains, and also the reference to the law of pairing (duality) in plants.

Messages

1- Not seeing something is not proof of its non-existence. (The heavens have pillars, even if we do not see them) «بغیر عمد ترونها»

2- Before anything else, stability and tranquility are prerequisites for life. «أن تمید بكم»

3- The system governing the earth is for the sake of humanity. «أن تمید بكم»

4- Water is a very great and important blessing. Water is the source of growth and nourishment for many creatures. («ماءً», is indefinite (nakirah) and carries a tanwin, which is a sign of its greatness.)

5- We should look generously upon plants, greenery, and the environment, because the Generous Allah, in the Noble Quran, has called them noble (karim). «من كلّ زوج كریم»

Ayah

هَـٰذَا خَلْقُ ٱللَّهِ فَأَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ بَلِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (۱۱)
This is the creation of Allah. Now show Me what others besides Him have created. Indeed, the wrongdoers are in manifest error! (11)

Messages

1- In discussions and conversations, we should use concrete examples. «هذا»

2- First, state the path of truth, and then criticize or struggle against the opponents of the truth. «هذا خلق اللّه فارونى ماذا»

3- One of the ways of knowing Allah is comparing His power with the power of others. «هذا خلق اللّه فارونى ماذا خلق الّذین من دونه»

4- We should not only listen to claims; we should demand proof, documentation, and examples. «فارونى»

5- Stubbornness and obstinacy against the truth constitute oppression (zulm). «بل الظالمون» (They see all these signs, yet they still harbor enmity toward Allah and the path of truth.)

6- Those who turn to other than Allah are both misguided and oppressive (zalim). «بل الظالمون فى ضلال»

7- The deviation of polytheism (shirk) is not hidden from anyone. «ضلال مبین»

Ayah

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَـٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (۱۲)
Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom, saying, ‘Give thanks to Allah; and whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful, [let him know that] Allah is indeed all-sufficient, all-laudable.’ (12)

Key Points

1- Wisdom (Hikmah) is a gift from Allah, not something acquired through effort. (Although the prerequisites for it can be acquired.) «و لقد آتینا لقمان الحكمة»

2- A special blessing requires special gratitude. «آتینا لقمان الحكمة... اشكر للّه» Just as Allah demanded special gratitude from the Prophet of Islam in return for granting him the special blessing of Kawthar. «انّا اعطیناك الكوثر . فصلّ لربّك و انحر»(209)

3- The first command given to Prophet Moses (عليه السلام) after prophethood was the command of prayer (Salat), and the first command given to Luqman after granting him wisdom was the command of gratitude. «ان اشكر لِلّه»

4- Gratitude for Allah’s blessings is for the benefit of the person himself. «و من یشكر فانّما یشكر لنفسه» (And whoever is grateful is grateful for [the benefit of] himself). Just as we read elsewhere: «لئن شكرتم لازیدنّكم»(210) (If you are grateful, I will surely increase you [in favor]).

Portrait of Luqman the Wise

In Tafsir Al-Mizan, there is a discussion about Luqman, a portion of which we quote:

The Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله said: Luqman was not a prophet, but he was a servant who thought deeply and had true faith in Allah. He loved God, and Allah also loved him and granted him wisdom.

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: Luqman did not receive wisdom because of wealth, beauty, or family, but rather he was a pious, sharp-sighted, modest, and compassionate man. If two people were in conflict and developed enmity, he would reconcile them.

Luqman frequently sat with scholars. He struggled against his own desires, and so on...(211)

He had a long life, was a contemporary of Prophet David (عليه السلام), and was related to Prophet Job (عليه السلام). He was given the choice between becoming a wise man (Hakim) or a ruler (Hakim), and he chose wisdom.

Luqman was asked: How did you reach this status? He replied: "Because of trustworthiness, truthfulness, and silence regarding what did not concern me."(212)

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: "Luqman had knowledge (Ma’rifah) of the heavenly leader of his time."(213)

Although Allah did not give him a heavenly book, He taught him its equivalent, which is wisdom.

One day, Luqman’s master asked him to bring the best part of a sheep. Luqman brought the sheep’s tongue. The next day, he said: Bring the worst part of it. Luqman again brought the sheep’s tongue. When his master asked the reason for this action, Luqman said: If the tongue moves and speaks in the way of truth, it is the best part of the body; otherwise, it will be the worst part.(214)

It is sufficient for Luqman’s greatness that Allah, His Messenger, and the infallible Imams علیهم السلام have narrated his counsels for others.

A Glimpse of Luqman’s Counsels

  • If you discipline yourself in childhood, you will benefit from it in adulthood.
  • Avoid laziness and sluggishness, dedicate a portion of your life to learning, and do not argue or debate with stubborn people.
  • Do not dispute with jurists (Fuqaha), do not befriend the immoral (Fasiq), do not take the immoral as a brother, and do not associate with accused individuals.
  • Fear only Allah and hope in Him. Let the fear and hope regarding Allah be equal in your heart.
  • Do not rely on or attach your heart to the world; consider the world as a bridge.
  • Know that on the Day of Judgment, you will be questioned about four things: your youth, in what path you spent it; your life, in what you consumed it; your wealth and possessions, how you acquired them and in what way you spent them.
  • Do not covet what is in the hands of others, and treat all people with good character.
  • Consult frequently with your travel companions and share your travel provisions among them.
  • If they consult you, sincerely offer them your compassion. If they ask you for help or a loan, assist them, and listen to the words of someone older than you.
  • Perform your prayer at the beginning of its time, and perform the prayer in congregation even under the most difficult circumstances.(215)
  • If you are in prayer, guard your heart.
  • If you are eating food, guard your throat.
  • If you are among people, guard your tongue.
  • Never forget Allah and death; but forget the kindness you do to people or the harm others do to you.(216)

Wisdom: Knowledge (Ma’rifah) and Obedience

Wisdom (Hikmah) is an insight that arises in the shadow of divine knowledge (Ma’rifah), and it can be attained through contemplation of the secrets of existence, and by reaching truth, light, and piety (Taqwa).

Imam Baqir عليه السلام said: "Wisdom is the same as deep understanding, knowledge, and profound recognition—a recognition that compels a person toward the servitude of Allah and the heavenly leader, and keeps him away from major sins."

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: "The head of wisdom is obedience to Allah."(217) Raghib, regarding the meaning of this word, says: "Wisdom is reaching the truth through knowledge and intellect."(218)

The Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله said: "I am the house of wisdom, and Ali is its gate. Whoever seeks wisdom must enter through this way." «انا دار الحكمة و علىّ بابها فمن اراد الحكمة فلیأت الباب»(219) In numerous narrations, the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام have been introduced as the gate and key to wisdom.(220)

The Value of Wisdom

One of the duties of the prophets is teaching the Book and wisdom. «و یعلّمهم الكتاب والحكمة»(221)

Wisdom is the equivalent of the heavenly Book, and whoever is granted wisdom has certainly been given abundant good. «یؤتى الحكمة من یشاء و من یؤت الحكمة فقد اوتى خیرا كثیرا»(222)

According to narrations, wisdom is like a light placed in the soul, and its effects become apparent in a person’s speech and behavior.(223)

Wisdom is an insight and foresight that, if present in a poor person, makes him more beloved in society than a rich person, and if present in a minor, gives him superiority over adults.(224)

Wisdom is the lost property of the believer(225), and its acquisition is so highly recommended that it has been said: Just as you pick up a valuable pearl from among the garbage, acquire wisdom even if it comes from your enemies.(226)

The wise person does not dwell on superficial and fleeting matters; his attention is focused on the outcome and result of affairs. He neither quarrels with his superiors nor belittles his subordinates. He does not speak without knowledge or awareness, and no contradiction is found in his speech or behavior.

He does not waste divine blessings and does not pursue futility. He loves people as he loves himself, desires for them whatever he desires for himself, and never deceives people.

How is Wisdom Acquired?

Wisdom is a gift that Allah bestows upon the sincere (Mukhlasin). In numerous narrations, we read: «مَن اَخلَصَ للّه اربعین صَباحاً ظَهرت یَنابیع الحكمة من قلبه على لسانه» (Whoever is sincere to Allah for forty mornings, the springs of wisdom will flow from his heart onto his tongue).(227)

In another Hadith, we read: The person who is ascetic (Zahid) in the world and is not deceived by its glitter, Allah establishes wisdom in his soul.(228) Guarding the tongue, controlling the stomach and desires, trustworthiness, humility, and abandoning useless matters are the grounds for the emergence of wisdom.(229)

Examples of Wisdom

In Surah Al-Isra, from Ayah 22 to Ayah 38, Allah has given commands, and at the end, He says: These commands and prohibitions are wisdoms that your Lord has revealed to you. These instructions include:

Monotheism and avoidance of polytheism (Shirk), kindness and respect toward parents, especially in old age, noble speech and humility accompanied by affection, praying for their well-being, paying the divine right to the friends of Allah, the poor, and the stranded traveler, avoiding extravagance and wastefulness, moderation in consumption, avoiding infanticide and unlawful sexual acts, avoiding encroachment on the property of the orphan, fulfilling the rights of the people and avoiding short-changing, not following that of which you have no knowledge or awareness, and avoiding walking arrogantly.

Then He says: «ذلك مما اوحى الیك ربّك من الحكمة» (That is from what your Lord has revealed to you of wisdom). These recommendations are examples of divine wisdom.

Ayah

وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَـٰنُ لِٱبْنِهِۦ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُۥ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ (۱۳)
When Luqman said to his son, as he advised him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice.’ (13)

Key Points

Admonition (Maw'izah) is one of the ways of inviting people to the truth, and no one is independent of it. One of the names of the Qur'an is Admonition. «قد جائتكم موعظة من ربّكم» (230) And in books of Hadith, a specific chapter dedicated to admonitions is found. In some narrations, it is stated that sometimes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would say to Gabriel: "Admonish me." (231) Imam Ali عليه السلام also sometimes told some of his companions: "Admonish me, for there is an effect in hearing that is not present in knowing." (232)

230) Yunus, 57. 231) Mawa'iz Saduq, p. 92. 232) Dah Guftar, Shahid Mutahhari, p. 224.

Messages

  1. Let us learn the methods of raising children from the elders. «و اذ قال لقمان لابنه»
  2. In admonition, we must first draw the listener's mind and attention to ourselves. «یا بنىّ»
  3. Admonition must be based on wisdom. «و لقد آتینا لقمان الحكمة... و هو یعظه»
  4. A child needs advice. Let us not neglect our children. «قال لقمان لابنه»
  5. In propagation, we should start with our close relatives. «قال لقمان لابنه»
  6. One of the best ways to properly raise a child is through sincere conversation with him. «و هو یعظه یا بنىّ»
  7. Admonition is one of the father's responsibilities toward his child. «قال لقمان لابنه و هو یعظه»
  8. One should speak to a teenager using the language of admonition, not reproach. «و هو یعظه یا بنىّ»
  9. Admonition must be accompanied by love and affection. «یا بنىّ»
  10. One of the conditions for the effectiveness of admonition is showing respect and giving dignity to the other party. «یا بنىّ»
  11. In guidance and admonition, we should prioritize the main issues. «یعظه... لاتشرك»
  12. The most important and central dimension of wisdom is Monotheism (Tawhid). «آتینا لقمان الحكمة... قال... لاتشرك»
  13. Shirk (polytheism/association) is the greatest danger and the most fundamental issue. In the sight of Allah, all the world is a small enjoyment, «متاع الدنیا قلیل» (233), but Shirk is a tremendous injustice. «لظلمٌ عظیم» This means that even if someone were offered the entire world to commit Shirk, they should not accept it.
  14. Let us express our admonitions with reason and logic. «لاتشرك... انّ الشرك لظلم عظیم»

Meaning of Shirk

Shirk has a broad and extensive meaning, the most prominent of which is Shirk in the sense of idol worship. According to the fatwa of jurists, this type of Shirk causes one to exit the fold of religion and leads to apostasy.

Shirk also has other meanings: unquestioning obedience to other than Allah, or following one's desires, regarding which the Qur'an states: «و لقد بعثنا فى كلّ امّة رسولاً ان اعبدوا اللّه و اجتنبوا الطاغوت» (234) (And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid the Taghut.") Or we read: «أفرأیت من اتّخذ الهه هواه» (235) (Have you seen he who has taken his desire as his god?)

The phrase «دون اللّه» (other than Allah) and «دونه» (other than Him) appears more than 100 times in the Qur'an, meaning that obedience and worship of other than Allah is Shirk. Believers also deviate from the path of Monotheism when they act contrary to divine commands and divine standards. «و ما یؤمن اكثرهم باللّه الاّ و هم مشركون» (236) (And most of them do not believe in Allah except while they associate others with Him.) This means that most believers are also polytheists (Mushrikun) and rely on non-divine supports.

It is stated in a narration that this type of Shirk is more hidden than the movement of an ant on a black stone on a dark night.

Therefore, Shirk is not only idol worship, but also dependence on any power, position, wealth, certificate, tribe, or any matter that is not in the direction of Allah.

Effects of Shirk

  1. Nullification of Deeds:

Shirk destroys a person's good deeds, just as fire destroys the green trees of a forest. The Qur'an addresses the Prophet, saying: «لئن اشركت لیحبطنّ عملك» (237) (If you associate [anything] with Allah, your work will surely become worthless.)

  1. Anxiety and Worry:

The goal of a God-worshipping, Monotheistic individual is to satisfy the One God, Who is easily satisfied. But whoever, instead of Allah, is concerned with satisfying others, is constantly afflicted by anxiety and worry. This is because people are numerous, and each has various demands and expectations.

Prophet Joseph عليه السلام said to his polytheist companions in prison: «أأرباب متفرّقون خیر أم اللّه الواحد القهّار» (238) (Are various lords better or Allah, the One, the Irresistible?)

The Qur'an states: The example of one who separates himself from Allah is like one who is thrown down from the sky and becomes prey for various birds, and every particle of him is scattered to a distant place. «و مَن یشرك باللّه فكانّما خَرَّ من السماء فتَخطَفه الطیَر او تَهوى به الرّیح فى مكان سَحیق» (239)

Yes, Monotheism and the pure servitude to Allah is a strong fortress that protects man from being wasted, from becoming attached to everyone, from revolving around everyone, from hoping in everyone, from flattering and praising everyone, and from fearing everyone. We read in a Hadith: «كلمة لااله الاّاللّه حِصنى فمن دَخل حِصنى أمِنَ من عذابى» (240) (The word 'La ilaha illa Allah' [There is no god but Allah] is My fortress, so whoever enters My fortress will be safe from My punishment.) Monotheism is a strong fortress and stronghold, and whoever enters it will be safe from the punishment of Allah.

  1. Disagreement and Division:

In a Monotheistic society, Allah is the axis of everything; Allah determines the leader, the law, and the path, and everyone revolves around that axis. But in a Shirk-ridden society, instead of the One God, there are multiple tyrants (Taghuts), tastes, and paths, causing people to fall into disagreement and division. The Qur'an states: «لاتكونوا من المشركین . من الّذین فرّقوا دینهم» (241) (And be not of the polytheists—[or] of those who divided their religion) [meaning] those who become agents of division (due to personal tastes and preconceived notions).

  1. Humiliation and Abasement on the Day of Judgment:

The Qur'an states: «لا تَجعل مع اللّه الهاً آخَرَ فتُلقى فى جهنّم مَلوماً مَدحوداً» (242) (And do not make [as equal] with Allah another deity, lest you be thrown into Hell, censured and banished.)

Signs of Shirk

One of the signs of Shirk is making excuses against divine law. The Qur'an refers to some of these, stating:

  • Do you act arrogantly whenever a messenger brings a law that does not align with your taste? «أفكلّما جاءكم رسول بما لاتهوى انفسكم استكبرتم» (243)
  • As soon as the command for Jihad arrived, they said: Why have You prescribed fighting for us? «لِمَ كتَبتَ علینا القِتال» (244)
  • When food arrived for a group of the Children of Israel, they said: Why this food? «لن نَصبِرَ على طعام واحد» (245)
  • Whenever Allah presented a parable, they said: What did Allah intend by this example? «ماذا اراد اللّه بهذا مثلاً» (246)

Another sign of Shirk is prioritizing family, wealth, position, etc., over the implementation of Allah's command. In Ayah 24 of Surah At-Tawbah, we read: If your fathers, your sons, your brothers, your spouses, your relatives, wealth, trade, and dwellings are dearer to you than Allah and His Messenger and striving in His way, then await the wrath of Allah.

Shirk is so dangerous that even though the Qur'an has commanded kindness to parents four times alongside Monotheism, if parents call their child to other than Allah, the Qur'an states that they should not be obeyed. (247)

Motivations for Shirk

People either turn to someone for power, regarding whom the Qur'an states: All people do not even have the power to create a fly, «لن یخلقوا ذباباً و لو اجتمعوا له» (248).

Or they turn to this person or that for a morsel of bread, regarding whom the Qur'an states: «لایملكون لكم رزقاً» (249) (They do not possess provision for you).

Or they turn to someone to attain honor, regarding whom the Qur'an states: «فانّ العزّة للّه جمیعاً» (250) (For indeed, honor belongs to Allah entirely).

Or they gather around someone for salvation from problems, regarding whom the Qur'an states: «فلایملكون كشف الضرّ عنكم» (251) (They do not possess [the power] to remove adversity from you).

And elsewhere, He states: Those whom you turn to instead of Allah are servants just like yourselves: «عباد امثالكم» (252). Why do you abandon the Best of Creators and pay attention to others? «تذرون احسن الخالقین» (253)

Combating Shirk

The first message and goal of all the Prophets was combating Shirk and inviting people to the pure servitude of Allah. «ولقد بعثنا فى كلّ اُمّة رسولاً اَنِ اعبدوا اللّه واجتنبوا الطاغوت» (254) (And We certainly sent into every nation a messenger, [saying], "Worship Allah and avoid the Taghut.")

All sins are subject to forgiveness, except Shirk. «اِنّ اللّه لا یغفر ان یشرك به و یغفر مادون ذلك» (255) (Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that.) The Prophets were commanded to clearly disavow all types of Shirk. (256)

Associating partners with Allah is rejected, even if the percentage is very small. If 99% of a deed is for Allah and only 1% is for other than Allah, the entire deed is void. As the Qur'an states: «و اعبدوا اللّه و لا تشركوا به شیئاً» (257) (And worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him.)

Not only idols and tyrants (Taghuts), but also the Prophets and the Divine Awliya (saints) should not be made partners with Allah. Allah says to Prophet Jesus: Did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as two gods besides Allah'? «أأنتَ قلتَ للناس اتّخذونى وامّى الهَین من دون اللّه» (258) Considering partners for Allah is slander, accusation, and a great sin. «و مَن یشرك باللّه فقد افترى اثماً عظيماً» (259)

Shirk is so detested that Allah states: The Prophet and the believers do not have the right to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they are their relatives. «ما كان للنبىّ والّذین آمنوا اَن یستغفروا للمشركین و لو كانوا أولى قُربى » (260)

Islam combats Shirk with reasoning and logic, stating: What have those besides Allah created that you have become attached to them?! «ماذا خلقوا من الارض» (261) (What have they created of the earth?) Who holds your death and life in His hand? Who holds your honor and abasement in His hand?

Yes, abandoning Allah, Who possesses infinite power and knowledge, and turning to individuals and objects that have no control over anything, is the greatest injustice to humanity. It is not wise to become a captive of inanimate objects and humans, to rely on industry, and to seek help from the incapable. One of the goals of recounting the stories and history of the ancients in the Qur'an is to eradicate the traces of Shirk.

At one time, the Shah of Iran suppressed the people of Iran by relying on superpowers, but it was not long before he fled weeping and in abasement. This is the meaning of the Ayah which states: Turning to other than Allah has no outcome but disgrace.

Ayah

وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ فِى عَامَيْنِ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ إِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (۱٤)
We have enjoined man concerning his parents: His mother carried him through weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years. Give thanks to Me and to your parents. To Me is the return. (14)

Key Points

The word wahn (وَهن) means physical weakness, just as tawhin (توهین) means the weakening of character (or insult/humiliation). The scope of ihsan (benevolence) is broader than infaq (spending/charity). Ihsan includes every type of affection and service, while infaq is usually used for financial aid. In the Noble Qur'an, ihsan towards parents is mentioned alongside Monotheism (Tawhid). «و قَضى ربّك اَلاّ تعبدوا اِلاّ ایّاه و بالوالدین احسانا»(262) In the current Ayah, first, kindness to parents is enjoined, and then the period of the mother's pregnancy is mentioned to stimulate and awaken the human moral conscience, reminding him not to forget the past. He should always remember that his mother carried him and gave him the essence of her life (sustenance), and sacrificed her sleep and food for his comfort—hardships that no one else would be willing to endure. Because the mother's right is more exposed to neglect, or because her right is greater than the father's, Allah has given a specific injunction. Parents and children have reciprocal rights. In the previous Ayah, the father's admonition to the son was discussed, and in this Ayah, the son's benevolence (ihsan) and gratitude towards the parents are discussed.

(262) Al-Isra, 23.

Messages

1- We should follow the word of truth, whether it is the admonition of a servant of Allah, like "Luqman," or the injunction of the Glorious Lord. «وصّینا» (We enjoined).

2- All human beings, regardless of their rank or condition, are indebted to their parents. «وصّینا الانسان» (We enjoined upon man). (Al-Insan includes all people.)

3- Respect for parents is a human right, not merely an Islamic one; even non-Muslim parents must be treated with ihsan (benevolence). «وصّینا الانسان بوالدیه»

4- Whoever endures greater hardship must be specially appreciated and their name mentioned separately. «والدیه - اُمّه» (His parents - His mother).

5- In general commands, the reasoning must also be general. (Since the audience is all human beings, the mother's pregnancy is mentioned, which relates to all people, as every human has a mother). «حملته اُمّه» (His mother carried him).

6- We must not shirk gratitude and benevolence toward someone on the pretext that their action is a natural individual duty. (Pregnancy is a natural matter, but we have the responsibility to respect the mother and appreciate her efforts). «حملته اُمّه»

7- We should recall the past so that the spirit of gratitude is revived within us. «حملته اُمّه»

8- The periods of pregnancy and breastfeeding are factors that establish the mother's rights over the child and are the most important periods for the formation of the child's character. «حملة اُمّه... و فصاله» (His mother carried him... and his weaning).

9- Weaning a child can occur within the two years, and it is not obligatory to wait until the end of the two years. (The word fisaal (فصال) means weaning the child from milk, and fi aamayn (فى عامین - in two years) indicates that the child can be weaned within the two years, although it is better for the full two years to be completed.)

10- Enduring hardship in the path of fulfilling a duty is the source of extraordinary rights. Those who bear the burden of responsibility despite weakness and fatigue have a greater right over man. «وهناً على وهن» (Weakness upon weakness).

11- Gratitude towards parents holds a high status with Allah. «ان اشكر لى و لوالدیك» (Be grateful to Me and to your parents). Gratitude towards parents is mentioned immediately after gratitude towards Allah.

12- The ultimate return of all of us is to Allah, so we must fear ingratitude towards our parents. «الىّ المصیر» (To Me is the return).

13- Belief in the Resurrection is a motivation for righteous deeds, including benevolence towards parents. «الىّ المصیر»

14- The right of Allah precedes the right of parents. «ان اشكر لى و لوالدیك» Gratitude and benevolence towards parents should not make us neglectful of Allah.

At the conclusion of the messages of this Ayah, we briefly touch upon two topics: benevolence towards parents and gratitude to Allah:

Benevolence towards Parents

In Ayahs 83 of Surah Al-Baqarah, 36 of Surah An-Nisa, 151 of Surah Al-An'am, and 23 of Surah Al-Isra, benevolence towards parents is enjoined, but in Surah Luqman, benevolence towards parents is presented as a Divine injunction.

In several instances in the Qur'an, gratitude towards parents is mentioned alongside gratitude towards Allah, and in some cases, the mother's exhausting efforts are specifically mentioned.

The status of parents is so high that the next Ayah states: If your parents strive to make you incline toward polytheism (shirk), do not obey them, but still do not cease your good conduct toward them. That is, even in cases where they should not be obeyed, they should not be abandoned.

Yes, respect for parents is a human right, not an Islamic right; it is a permanent right, not a seasonal or temporary one.

In narrations, we read that benevolence towards parents is necessary in all circumstances, whether they are good or bad, alive or deceased.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was seen showing more respect to his foster sister than his foster brother. When asked the reason, the Prophet said: "Because this sister shows more respect to her parents."(263)

In the Qur'an, we read that Prophets Yahya and Isa علیهما السلام were both commanded to respect their mothers.(264)

The Prophet Muhammad صلى الله علیه وآله said: "After the prayer at its prescribed time, there is no deed better than respecting parents."(265)

In Karbala, a father was martyred, and his son rose to help the Imam. Imam Husayn عليه السلام told the son: "Go back, because your mother has suffered the grief of her husband and may not be able to bear the grief of losing you." The young man replied: "My mother sent me to the battlefield."(266)

The Scope of the Meaning of Parents

In Islamic culture, the heavenly leader, the teacher and educator, and the father of the spouse are also called "father."

During the final days of the Prophet's life, when he was ill in bed, he told Ali عليه السلام: Go among the people and say loudly:

The curse of Allah be upon anyone who is undutiful to his parents (‘aqq al-walidayn),

The curse of Allah be upon any slave who flees from his master,

The curse of Allah be upon anyone who does not give the right of the hired worker (ajir).

Imam Ali عليه السلام went and spoke these sentences among the people and returned. Some companions considered this message simple and asked: We had previously heard such statements regarding respect for parents, the master, and the hired worker; this was not a new message for the Prophet to send us from His sickbed. When the Prophet realized that the people had not grasped the depth of His message, He sent Ali عليه السلام again and commanded: Tell the people:

My intention by ‘aqq al-walidayn (undutifulness to parents) is undutifulness to the heavenly leader. O Ali! You and I are the fathers of this Ummah, and whoever does not obey us is undutiful to us.

You and I are the masters (mawla) of these people, and those who flee from us are subject to Allah's wrath.

You and I have been hired (ajir) for the guidance of these people, and those who do not give the right of the hired worker are subject to Allah's curse.

In this incident, you observe that although benevolence and obedience to parents have a well-known and formal meaning, the issue is broader in Islamic culture.

Gratitude to Allah

Allah has no need for our worship or gratitude, and the Qur'an has repeatedly pointed to this truth, stating: Allah is independent of you.(267) But our attention to Him is a source of our own honor and growth, just as the sun has no need for us; it is we who benefit from its light and brightness if we build our homes facing the sun.

One of the praises Allah gives to the Prophets in the Qur'an is having a spirit of gratitude. For example, concerning Prophet Noah, despite all the patience and steadfastness he endured against the disloyalty of his wife, son, and people, Allah mentions his gratitude and calls him grateful. «كان عبداً شكوراً» (He was a grateful servant).(268)

Allah has often complained about the ingratitude of most people.

Of course, the ability to thank Allah must be sought from Him, just as Prophet Solomon requested this from Him: «ربّ اوزعنى أن أشكر نعمتك الّتى انعمت علىّ» (My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me).(269) We usually only pay attention to the blessings we deal with every day and are heedless of many blessings, including those that have reached us through ancestors and inheritance, or the thousands of calamities that are averted from us every moment, or spiritual blessings, such as faith in Allah and His Awliya (saints), or aversion to disbelief (kufr), immorality (fisq), and sin, which Allah mentions in the Qur'an: «حبّب الیكم الایمان و زیّنه فى قلوبكم و كرّه الیكم الكفر و الفسوق و العصیان» (He has endeared to you the faith and adorned it in your hearts, and made hateful to you disbelief, immorality, and disobedience).(270)

In addition to what we have stated, a portion of the supplications (du'as) of the Infallibles (Ma'sumin) is dedicated to reflecting on Divine blessings and expressing gratitude for them, in order to revive and strengthen the spirit of thankfulness in man.

Gratitude to Allah is sometimes through the tongue and speech, and sometimes through action and behavior.

In a Hadith, we read: Whenever you recall one of Allah's blessings, prostrate and place your face on the ground in thanks for it. Even if you are riding a horse, dismount and perform this act, and if you cannot, place your face on the high part of the saddle, and if you cannot do even that, place your face on the palm of your hand and thank Allah.(271)

Examples of Practical Gratitude

  1. Prayer (Salat): Prayer is the best example of gratitude to Allah. Allah tells His Prophet: In thanks for Our giving you Al-Kawthar (abundant good), establish prayer. «انّا أعطیناك الكوثر . فصلّ لربّك و انحر» (Indeed, We have granted you Al-Kawthar. So pray to your Lord and sacrifice [to Him]).(272)

  2. Fasting: Just as the Divine Prophets used to fast in thanks for Allah's blessings.(273)

  3. Service to People: The Qur'an states: If an illiterate person asks a literate person to write, he should not refuse, and in thanks for his literacy, he should write the letter for him. «ولا یأب كاتب أن یكتب كما علّمه اللّه» (And let no scribe refuse to write as Allah has taught him). (274) Here, writing a letter, which is service to people, is considered a type of gratitude for the blessing of literacy.

  4. Contentment (Qana'ah): The Prophet Muhammad صلى الله علیه وآله said: «كن قَنِعاً تكن اشكر النّاس» (Be content, and you will be the most grateful of people).(275)

  5. Caring for Orphans: Allah tells His Prophet: In thanks for the fact that you were an orphan and We gave you shelter, do not repel the orphan. «فامّا الیتیم فلاتقهر» (So as for the orphan, do not oppress [him]).(276)

  6. Helping the Needy: Allah tells His Prophet: In thanks for the fact that you were needy and We enriched you, do not repel the beggar who comes to you. «و امّا السائل فلاتنهر» (And as for the beggar, do not repel [him]).(277)

  7. Thanking People: Allah tells His Prophet: To thank and encourage those who pay Zakat (charity), send blessings upon them, for your blessings are a source of tranquility for them. «و صلّ علیهم انّ صلاتك سكن لهم» (And invoke blessings upon them; indeed, your invocation is a source of tranquility for them).(278)

Thanking people is thanking Allah, just as it is stated in the narration: Whoever does not thank the benefactor among the creatures has not thanked Allah. «مَن لم یشكر المنعم من المخلوقین لم یشكر اللّه»(279)

Bitterness also Requires Gratitude

The Qur'an states: «عسى أن تكرهوا شیئاً و هو خیر لكم و عسى أن تحبّوا شیئاً و هو شرّ لكم» (Perhaps you dislike a thing and it is good for you; and perhaps you love a thing and it is bad for you).(280)

If we know: That others have greater problems. That problems increase our attention to Allah. That problems break our pride and remove our hard-heartedness. That problems remind us of the suffering. That problems make us think about defense and innovation. That problems remind us of the value of past blessings. That problems are expiations for sins. That problems are a cause for receiving rewards in the Hereafter. That problems are a warning and a wake-up call for the Day of Judgment. That problems lead to the recognition of our own patience or the recognition of true friends. And if we know that greater or harder problems could have befallen us, we will realize that apparent bitterness is sweet in its own place.

Yes, for a child, dates are sweet, and onions and peppers are pungent and unpleasant; but for parents who have greater maturity and insight, both sour and sweet are pleasant.

Imam Ali عليه السلام said during the Battle of Uhud: Participation in the front lines is among the instances for which gratitude must be performed. «و لكن من مواطن البشرى و الشكر»(281) And his daughter, Lady Zaynab علیها السلام, in response to the criminals of Bani Umayyah, said: I saw nothing but beauty in Karbala. «ما رأیت الاّ جمیلاً»(282)

It was said to one of Allah's saints (awliya): Fulfill the right of gratitude to Allah. He said: I am incapable of thanking and praising Him. The response came: The best gratitude is precisely this, that you confess, "I do not have the ability to thank Him." Sa'di says:

By whose hand and tongue can it be done To fulfill the obligation of His gratitude? It is better for the servant that, due to his shortcomings, He brings an excuse to the threshold of God.

Ayah

وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا ۖ وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا ۖ وَٱتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَىَّ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (۱٥)
But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. Keep their company honourably in this world and follow the way of those who turn to Me penitently. Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do. (15)

Key Points

In the Quran, we have three types of obedience: 1. Absolute obedience to Allah, the Prophet, and those in authority (ulul-amr). «اطیعوا اللّه و اطیعوا الرسول و اولى الامر منكم»(283) 2. Non-absolute obedience to disbelievers (kuffar), hypocrites (munafiqin), corrupt individuals (mufsidun), sinners, oppressors (zalimin), and others. «و لاتطع الكافرین و المنافقین»(284), «لاتطع منهم آثماً أو كفوراً»(285), «لا تتّبع سبیل المفسدین»(286) 3. Conditional obedience to parents; meaning, if they issue beneficial or permissible commands, it is necessary to follow them; but if they strive to call the child towards other than Allah, obedience is forbidden.

283) Nisa, 59. 284) Ahzab, 1. 285) Insan, 24. 286) Sad, 26.

Messages

  1. Man naturally possesses a monotheistic inclination, and others strive to make him commit polytheism (shirk). «جاهداك»

  2. Alongside stating general principles, we must also pay attention to exceptions and provisos. (Alongside the command to obey parents, «وَصّینا الانسان», attention is also given to the potential deviations of the parents). «جاهداك على ان تشرك»

  3. Polytheism (shirk) has no scientific logic whatsoever. «لیس لك به علم»

  4. Blind imitation is forbidden. «ما لیس لك به علم فلا تطعهما»

  5. The right of Allah takes precedence over every other right, including the right of parents. «فلاتطعهما»

  6. Whenever we adopt a role model from someone, we should replace it with a suitable alternative role model. «فلاتطعهما - واتبع»

  7. In cases of deviation, obedience to parents is not necessary, but ordinary life (interaction) must be maintained. «فلا تطعهما و صاحبهما فى الدنیا معروفا»

  8. Peaceful coexistence with non-Muslims is permissible, provided their culture is not followed. «فلاتطعهما و صاحبهما... معروفا»

  9. Good deeds and virtuous conduct (ma'ruf) must never be abandoned under any circumstances. «صاحبهما فى الدنیا معروفا»

  10. One must treat parents kindly until the end of life. (Even polytheistic parents). «صاحبهما فى الدنیا معروفا»

  11. Godly individuals are worthy of being role models. «واتّبع سبیل مَن اناب الىّ»

  12. Sometimes, separation is necessary in choosing belief and conduct within the family; in worldly life, accompany the parents, but in following (a path), follow the way of the pure and the righteous. «واتّبع سبیل مَن اَناب الىّ»

  13. Belief in the Resurrection (Ma'ad) guarantees the rectification of affairs and man's obedience to the path of Allah. «الىَّ مرجعكم»

  14. The Day of Judgment is the arena where humans will be present before the Divine Presence. «الىَّ مرجعكم فاُنبّئكم»

Ayah

يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنَّهَآ إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِى صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَوْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ (۱٦)
‘O my son! Even if it should be the weight of a mustard seed, and [even though] it should be in a rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth, Allah will produce it. Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (16)

Key Points

"Mustard" (خردل) is a plant with black, very small seeds, whose seeds are proverbial for their minuteness and insignificance.

Messages

1- Directing one's child toward divine knowledge and power is a sign of wisdom. «آتینا لقمان الحكمة... یا بنىّ...»

2- In admonition, we should repeat the name of the addressee for every piece of advice. (The repetition of «یا بُنىّ» in this Surah and the repetition of «یا ابت» in Surah Maryam, verses 40 to 45).

3- A person's faith in the presence of his deeds on the Day of Judgment is the source of his reformation. «اِن تك مثقال حبّة... یأت بها اللّه»

4- In a complete evaluation and supervision, even the smallest deeds must be taken into consideration. «مثقال حبّة من خردل»

5- Smallness («خَردَل»), hardness («صَخرَة»), distance, and invisibility («السّموات, الارض») have no effect on divine knowledge or the power to bring forth the deed. «یأت بها اللّه»

6- Allah is aware of everything and capable of everything. «یأت بها اللّه... لطیف خبیر»

7- Human deeds are not lost in this world. «یأت بها اللّه»

8- Allah’s accountability is precise because He is Subtle (Latif). «لطیفٌ خبیر»

Ayah

يَـٰبُنَىَّ أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱنْهَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ ۖ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (۱۷)
O my son! Maintain the prayer and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and be patient through whatever may befall you. That is indeed the steadiest of courses. (17)

Key Points

The meaning of ‘azm (determination) here is either the firm determination and will of Allah to carry out these matters, or the necessity for serious determination and resolve on the part of man to carry them out. Although Zakat (alms) is mentioned 28 times alongside Salat (prayer) in the Quran, here Amr bil Ma’ruf (enjoining good) is mentioned alongside Salat. Perhaps this is because the addressee in this Ayah is the son, and sons usually do not possess wealth to pay Zakat. «أقم الصّلاة وامر بالمعروف» (Establish the prayer and enjoin good). Imam Ali عليه السلام says: Be patient regarding the hardships and difficulties that befall you in the path of enjoining good and forbidding evil. (287) Patience is sometimes in the face of calamity, «و بشّرالصّابرین . الّذین اذا أصابتهم مصیبة» (And give good tidings to the patient, who, when disaster strikes them...) (288). Sometimes it is in the face of performing a duty, «وامر بالمعروف و انه عن المنكر واصبر على ما أصابك» (And enjoin good and forbid evil and be patient over what befalls you). And sometimes it is in the face of sin, just as Joseph said in prison: «ربّ السّجن أحبّ الىّ...» (My Lord, prison is more to my liking...) O Lord! Prison is better for me than committing the sin to which they invite me, until He said: «انّه مَن یتّق و یصبر فانّ اللّه لا یضیع اجر المحسنین» (Indeed, he who fears Allah and is patient, then indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of the doers of good.) (289)

287) Tafsir Majma‘ al-Bayan. 288) Al-Baqarah, 155-156. 289) Yusuf, 90.

Messages

  1. One of the duties of parents towards their children is to enjoin them to prayer. «یا بُنىّ أَقِم الصّلاة»
  2. Let us begin inner purification by avoiding polytheism (shirk) and spiritual reconstruction with prayer. «لاتشرك... اقم الصلاة»
  3. The obligation of enjoining good and prayer is not exclusive to the religion of Islam. (Even before Islam, Luqman enjoined his son to prayer). «یا بُنىّ أقم الصّلاة وامر بالمعروف»
  4. Let us raise our children to be faithful and godly through the injunction to prayer, and responsible and social individuals through the injunction to enjoin good and forbid evil. «یا بُنىّ أقم الصّلاة وامر بالمعروف»
  5. We must raise our children's awareness enough so that they recognize good deeds (Ma’ruf) and evil deeds (Munkar) in order to be able to enjoin and forbid. «یا بُنىّ - وَ امُر - وَ انْهَ»
  6. After attention to the Origin (Allah) and the Resurrection, the most important act is prayer. «أقم الصّلاة»
  7. Whoever wishes to enjoin and forbid in society, and consequently clash with people's desires, must purify himself through prayer and quench his thirst with faith. «أقم الصّلاة وامر بالمعروف»
  8. Let us raise our children to be enjoiners of good and forbidder of evil. «وامر بالمعروف...»
  9. Enjoining good is always before forbidding evil. «وامر بالمعروف وانه عن المنكر» The growth of good deeds in society prevents the occurrence of many evils.
  10. Being on the path of truth is not enough; it is necessary to invite others to the path of truth as well. «وامر بالمعروف» Just as we read in Surah Al-Asr: «الّذین آمنوا و عملوا الصّالحات و تواصوا بالحقّ» (Those who have believed and done righteous deeds and advised one another to truth).
  11. In propagating the religion and implementing the duty of enjoining good and forbidding evil, we must have patience and forbearance. «واصبر على ما أصابك» Forbidding evil is accompanied by the bitterness and stings of sinners; we must not retreat.
  12. The wise father accepts that hardship may reach his son and commands patience, but he never accepts a blow to his school of thought, which would be silence in the face of corruption. «و انه عن المنكر و اصبر على ما أصابك»
  13. Enjoining good and forbidding evil are important issues, and patience in the face of what befalls a person in this path is valuable. «انّ ذلك من عزم الامور»

Portrait of Prayer

Due to the injunction regarding prayer and enjoining good in this Ayah, we now offer a brief explanation concerning these two duties:

  • Prayer is the simplest, deepest, and most beautiful relationship between man and Allah, and it has existed in all divine religions.
  • Prayer is the only act of worship for which it is enjoined that the most melodious individuals ascend to a high place and loudly proclaim the slogan: «حىّ على الصّلاة، حىّ على الفلاح، حىّ على خیرالعمل» (Hasten to the Prayer, Hasten to Salvation, Hasten to the Best of Deeds). With their Adhan (call to prayer), they break the silence, announce a complete set of pure Islamic thoughts, and awaken the heedless.
  • Prayer is so important that Prophet Abraham عليه السلام introduced the establishment of prayer—not the performance of Hajj rituals—as His goal for settling His wife and son in the barren desert of Mecca.
  • Imam Husayn عليه السلام, at noon on Ashura, offered His chest as a shield against the enemy's arrows in order to establish two units (rak’ahs) of prayer.
  • The Quran commands Prophets Abraham and Ishmael عليهما السلام to prepare and purify the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) for those who pray. Yes, prayer is so important that Zechariah, Mary, Abraham, and Ishmael عليهم السلام were servants of the mosque and the place where prayer was established.
  • Prayer is the key to the acceptance of all deeds, and the Commander of the Faithful, Imam Ali عليه السلام, told His governor: Dedicate your best time to prayer, and be aware that all your affairs are accepted in the light of your prayer. (290)
  • Prayer is the remembrance of Allah, and the remembrance of Allah is the only solace for hearts.
  • Prayer is discussed in most chapters of the Quran, from the largest Surah (Al-Baqarah) to the smallest (Al-Kawthar).
  • Prayer manifests from the moment of birth in the delivery room, when the Adhan and Iqamah are recited in the infant's ear, until after death, when the prayer is performed over the deceased in the cemetery.
  • Prayer of Signs (Salat al-Ayat) has been made obligatory both for terrestrial events like earthquakes and frightening winds, and for celestial events like lunar and solar eclipses. Even the Prayer for Rain (Salat al-Istisqa) has been prescribed.
  • Prayer prevents man from committing many evils and indecencies. (291)

All perfections can be seen in prayer; we mention a few examples:

  • We see cleanliness and hygiene in brushing teeth, Wudu (ablution), Ghusl (ritual bath), and the purity of the body and clothing.
  • We learn courage, boldness, and proclamation from the Adhan.
  • We learn presence on the scene from gathering in the mosques.
  • We find attention to justice in the selection of a just Imam for the congregation.
  • We gain attention to values and perfections from those who stand in the first row.
  • We feel independent orientation in the Qibla (direction of prayer). Yes, Jews perform their worship facing one direction and Christians another, but Muslims must be independent, and thus, by the command of the Quran, the Kaaba became the independent Qibla of the Muslims, so that their independence in orientation is preserved.
  • We see the observance of the rights of others in the fact that not even a single thread obtained unjustly should be in the clothing of the worshipper.
  • We feel attention to politics where we read in narrations that prayer is not accepted unless it is accompanied by the acceptance of the Wilayah (guardianship) of the infallible Imam.
  • We see attention to order in the organized rows of congregational prayer, attention to martyrs in the soil of Karbala, and attention to environmental health in the recommendations given for the purity and cleanliness of the mosque and those who attend it.
  • We see attention to Allah throughout the prayer, attention to the Resurrection in «مالك یوم الدین» (Master of the Day of Judgment), attention to choosing the path in «اهدنا الصراط المستقیم» (Guide us to the straight path), choosing good companions in «صراط الّذین انعمت علیهم» (The path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor), avoidance of the deviated and the wrathful in «غیر المغضوب علیهم و لاالضّالّین» (Not of those who have incurred [Your] wrath or of those who are astray), attention to prophethood and the Prophet's Household صلى الله عليه وآله in the Tashahhud (testimony), and attention to the pure and righteous in «السلام علینا و على عباد اللّه الصالحین» (Peace be upon us and upon the righteous servants of Allah).
  • We see attention to healthy nutrition where it is mentioned in a Hadith (narration) that if someone consumes alcoholic beverages, his prayer is not accepted for forty days.
  • We see attention to outward appearance where it is recommended to use the best clothing, perfume, and adornment in prayer, and even for women to wear their ornaments during prayer.
  • We see attention to one's spouse where we read in a Hadith that if there is bitterness between a husband and wife, or if they hurt each other or speak ill, the prayer of neither is accepted.

These are but a few of the effects and considerations that we are reminded of during prayer. Imam Khomeini قدس سره said: "Prayer is a factory for building human beings."

Enjoining Good and Forbidding Evil

Enjoining good means recommending good deeds, and forbidding evil means restraining from bad deeds.

The performance of these two important duties does not require a specific age, as Luqman tells his son: «یا بنّى أقم الصّلاة و امر بالمعروف...»

Enjoining good is a sign of love for the school (of thought), love for people, concern for the health of society, and a sign of freedom of expression, religious zeal, friendly communication between people, a vigilant nature, public oversight, and presence on the scene.

Enjoining good and forbidding evil leads to the encouragement of the righteous, the enlightenment of the ignorant, a warning to prevent transgression, and the creation of a kind of social discipline. The Quran says: You Muslims are the best nation because you enjoin good and forbid evil. (292)

Imam Ali عليه السلام says: Enjoining good and forbidding evil is for the public interest. (293) Just as we read in another Hadith: Whoever does not prevent evil is like someone who abandons an injured person on the road to die. (294)

Prophets such as David and Jesus عليهما السلام have cursed those who do not forbid evil. (295)

The uprising of Imam Husayn عليه السلام was for the purpose of enjoining good and forbidding evil. «انما خرجتُ لطلب الاصلاح فى امّة جدّى ارید ان آمر بالمعروف و انهى عن المنكر» (I only rose up seeking reform in the nation of my grandfather; I want to enjoin good and forbid evil.) (296)

In a Hadith, we read: Through enjoining good and forbidding evil, all obligatory duties are upheld. (297)

The Quran says: If Allah's verses are being insulted in a gathering, leave the session as a protest until the course of the discussion changes. (298)

A person must be displeased in his heart about the commission of sin, forbid it with his tongue, and prevent its occurrence through the application of power and law.

If we invite someone to a good deed, we share in the reward of his good deeds, but if we remain silent in the face of corruption, deviation, and sin, corruption will gradually grow, and the corrupt and mischief-makers will rule over the people.

Silence and indifference in the face of sin cause sinning to become normal, the sinner to become emboldened, us to become hard-hearted, Satan to be pleased, and Allah to be wrathful toward us.

Enjoining good and forbidding evil are two divine duties, and delusions such as:

The sins of others are none of our business, we should not restrict people's freedom, I am afraid and shy, one flower does not make spring, Jesus to his religion and Moses to his, we will not be placed in the same grave, others are there, why should I enjoin good? By forbidding evil, I will lose my friends or customers—and similar excuses—cannot remove this obligation from our shoulders. Of course, enjoining good and forbidding evil must be done knowingly, compassionately, wisely, and, as far as possible, secretly.

Sometimes we must speak ourselves, but where our words have no effect, the duty is not dropped; rather, we must ask others to speak.

Even if corruption can be stopped for a short time, it must be stopped, and if results can be achieved through repetition, it must be repeated.

Ayah

وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (۱۸)
Do not turn your cheek away disdainfully from the people, and do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (18)

Key Points

"تَصعیر" (Taṣ‘īr) is a type of disease that afflicts camels, causing their necks to become twisted. Luqmān tells his son: Do not twist your neck away from people out of arrogance, like a sick camel. "مَرَح" (Maraḥ) means excessive joy acquired due to wealth and status. "مُختال" (Mukhtāl) refers to someone who considers himself superior based on imagination and delusion, and "فَخور" (Fakhūr) means boastful. Arrogance is both an insult to people, a ground for the growth of new resentments, and a stimulant for old inner grudges. We read in a tradition: Whoever walks upon the earth with arrogance, the earth and everything above and below it curse him.(299)

299) Tafsir Nūr al-Thaqalayn.

Messages

1- We should treat people, whether Muslim or non-Muslim, with cheerfulness. "لاتصعّر خدّك للنّاس"

2- Arrogance is forbidden, even in walking. "لاتمش فى الارض مرحا"

3- We should use the factor of Allah's pleasure for encouragement and His displeasure for abandoning indecencies and sins. "انّ اللّه لایحبّ"

4- We should not entangle ourselves in delusions, fantasies, and high ambitions. "مُختال"

5- We should not boast to people. "انّ اللّه لایحبّ كلّ مُختال فَخور"

Humility

In this Ayah, Luqmān tells his son: Do not walk upon the earth arrogantly, "لاتمش فى الارض مرحاً" (Do not walk upon the earth exultantly). And in Sūrah Al-Furqān, the first sign of Allah's good servants is their humble movement: "و عباد الرّحمن الّذین یمشون على الارض هَونا" (And the servants of the Most Merciful are those who walk upon the earth humbly).(300)

One of the secrets of prayer (Salat), where in prostration (Sujud) we place the highest point of the body (at least 34 times in seventeen obligatory Rak'ahs every day and night) upon the dust, is to distance oneself from arrogance and pride, and to show humility before Allah.

Although humility is necessary before all human beings, it is even more necessary before parents, teachers, and believers. Humility before believers is one of the prominent signs of the people of faith. "اذّلة على المؤمنین" (Humble toward the believers).(301)

Contrary to the demand of the arrogant, who suggested that the prophets should drive away the poor, those great figures said: We will never drive them away. "و ما انا بطارد الّذین آمنوا" (And I am not one to drive away those who have believed).(302)

Why does a weak and helpless human being, created from dust and a drop of semen, who will be nothing more than a corpse in the future, act arrogantly?!

Is not his limited knowledge vulnerable to forgetfulness?!

Are not his beauty, power, fame, and wealth perishable?!

Has he not seen sickness, poverty, and death in society?!

Are not his abilities subject to destruction?!

So why does he act arrogantly?! The Quran says: Do not walk arrogantly, for you will not pierce the earth, nor will you stretch your neck so high that you become taller than the mountains. "ولاتَمشِ فى الارض مَرحاً اِنّك لن تَخرق الارض و لن تَبلغ الجبال طولا" (And do not walk upon the earth exultantly. Indeed, you will never tear the earth [apart], and you will never reach the mountains in height).(303)

Examples of the Humility of the Divine Saints

  1. The foremost person in creation, the Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله, would sit in such a way that He had no distinction over others, and people entering the gathering who did not know Him would ask: Which one of you is the Messenger of Allah? "ایّكم رسول اللّه" (Which of you is the Messenger of Allah?).(304)

  2. During a journey where everyone took responsibility for a task to prepare food, the Prophet also took on a task and said: "Collecting the firewood is my responsibility."(305)

  3. When the Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله saw that there was no mat for some people to sit on, He gave His cloak so that a few people could sit on His cloak.(306)

Wearing simple clothes, riding a bare donkey, milking, sitting with slaves, greeting children, patching shoes and clothes, accepting people's invitations, sweeping the house, shaking hands with everyone, and not belittling food were among the Prophet's practices.(307)

  1. Some Shī‘ah wanted to empty the public bath (Hammam) and, in effect, reserve it out of respect for Imam Sadiq عليه السلام. The Imam did not permit it and said: "لا حاجة لى فى ذلك المؤمن اَخفّ من ذلك" (I have no need for that; the life of a believer is simpler than such formalities).(308)

  2. No matter how much they insisted that the dining cloth (Sufrah) of Imam Riḍā عليه السلام be separate from that of the servants, the Imam did not accept.(309)

  3. A person in the public bath did not recognize Imam Riḍā عليه السلام and asked Him to scrub him with a bath mitt. The Imam accepted his request with complete dignity and without introducing Himself. As soon as the person recognized the Imam, he began apologizing, but the Imam comforted him.(310)

Among the signs of humility are accepting the suggestions and criticisms of others, and sitting in a place lower than what is appropriate for one's status.
